---
title: "Green Gap Affordable Housing"
subtitle: "Spatial analysis of the green gap in affordable housing: DC and Maryland"
---

## About this project

This site publishes the analysis notebooks for the **Green Gap Affordable Housing**
project: a spatial investigation of how green space (from land cover / land use rasters)
relates to affordable-housing locations (LIHTC and NHPD) across Washington, DC and Maryland.

The analysis combines:

- **Parcels**: February 2026 parcel geometries
- **Land cover / land use**: DC and MD raster editions (2017–2022 / 2018–2022)
- **Affordable housing**: LIHTC and NHPD (National Housing Preservation Database)

## Interactive dashboard

**[→ Green Gap dashboard](dashboard/)** is an interactive bivariate map crossing a summer
environmental measure against LIHTC affordable units, for Maryland and Washington, DC.

Choose the unit of analysis (census block group or tract) and the environmental measure:
summer surface temperature (Landsat), tree canopy percent (Chesapeake land cover), NDVI, or
NDBI. Filter by jurisdiction, switch the LIHTC measure and classification scheme, and read off
the corner cell, where the most affordable housing coincides with the worst environmental value.

The map is descriptive. It shows where affordable housing and these conditions co-locate across
areal units. It does not compare LIHTC to market-rate housing, so it is not evidence of a
disparity or a causal effect. See **[Data & methods](data-methods.qmd)** for how every variable
was built and what it can and cannot support.

The dashboard runs entirely in your browser (WebAssembly), so there is no server and nothing to
install.

## Housing type and environment

**[→ Housing type & environment dashboard](dashboard-housing/)** is a second, parcel-scale view
that adds the comparison the areal dashboard cannot make. It sorts multifamily rental buildings
into **subsidised**, **NOAH** (naturally occurring affordable housing), and **market-rate**, then
contrasts them on the tree canopy and summer heat measured at each building's own footprint. NOAH
— unsubsidised stock that rents affordably without a program — is inferred from the assessor's
value per unit, using public data only.

It has five tabs: an environmental choropleth with buildings coloured by type, a block-group
choropleth of units by type, the per-state type contrast, a value-per-unit-versus-environment
scatter, and an adjusted regression showing each type's gap against market-rate before and after
controls.

See **[Data & methods](data-methods.qmd#housing-type-and-environment-parcel-scale-dashboard)** for
how buildings are defined, how the three types are labelled, and what the type contrast does and
does not show. The contrast is descriptive: the types differ in siting, age, and neighbourhood, so
a gap describes where they sit, not an effect of housing type. The relationship also differs
between Maryland and DC, which is a reason to read the two jurisdictions separately.

## Notebooks

- [1.0 Green gap demo](notebooks/1.0-example-green-gap-demo.ipynb):
  a worked example showing a `plotnine` chart and an interactive **bivariate choropleth** map.

## Reproducing the analysis

```bash
# install the environment (uv)
uv sync

# launch jupyter lab
uv run jupyter lab

# render this site locally
uv run quarto preview
```

## Data

Raw data lives under `data/raw/` (study inputs) and `data/external/` (third-party rasters), and
is **not** committed to the repository (see `.gitignore`). The one exception is
`app/units.geojson`, the small derived table the dashboard reads, because CI cannot rebuild it:
that needs the 2.4 GB land-cover rasters. See `references/` for provenance and data
dictionaries.
